projects.name: Cedar LNG
projects.summary: ≈ 3.3 mtpa floating LNG export facility at Kitimat, BC. Haisla Nation 50.1% / Pembina Pipeline 49.9% — the world's first Indigenous majority-owned LNG facility. Positive FID declared June 25, 2024; operations anticipated late 2028.
projects.location: Kitimat, BC (Haisla Nation territory; Douglas Channel)
projects.proponent: Cedar LNG Partners LP (Haisla Nation 50.1%, Pembina Pipeline Corporation 49.9%)
projects.governmentObjective: Demonstrate Indigenous-led major-project ownership at scale; expand Pacific LNG export capacity using BC Hydro renewable power as the basis for one of the lowest-carbon-intensity LNG facilities in the world.
projects.proponentObjective: Generate long-term LNG export revenue under a 20-year take-or-pay tolling agreement with ARC Resources Ltd. and a mirror agreement (currently held by Pembina) for the remaining 1.5 mtpa.

parties.name: Pembina Pipeline Corporation (TSX: PPL; NYSE: PBA)
Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (Strategic Innovation Fund — $200M)
Province of BC ($200M electrification contribution agreement)

firstNationImplications.text: Haisla Nation holds 50.1% equity — not an impact-benefit beneficiary but the majority owner. The Canada Energy Regulator's 2026 Market Snapshot frames Cedar LNG as the exemplar of Indigenous communities being involved 'during project development' rather than acquiring already-operating assets.

The Haisla equity raise is financially advised by CIBC and supported by the federal Strategic Innovation Fund ($200M) and a First Nations Finance Authority capital commitment; CER aggregates federal committed capital at ≈ $400M.

sources.citing: Pembina Apr 4 2024 release — CIBC as Haisla's financial advisor

firstNationImplications.text: Project crosses multiple Indigenous Nations' interests via shipping in Douglas Channel; engagement framework on the October 2024 EA amendments explicitly includes 'Haisla Nation and other Indigenous Nations and the community'.

Long-run effects on Haisla governance capacity, intergenerational wealth, and exposure to global LNG price volatility will be material over the project's operating life — material in proportion to Pembina's disclosed run-rate adjusted EBITDA guidance of US$200M–US$260M per year net to its 50% share.

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Liquefaction is powered by renewable electricity from BC Hydro — proponent characterizes Cedar LNG as 'one of the lowest carbon intensity LNG facilities in the world'.

October 2024 amendment package proposes an alternate transmission line re-route 'to avoid impacts on mature trees, marbled murrelet habitat, and wetlands', plus a previously-unapproved distribution power line, and an expanded marine terminal area following a switch from strut to spread mooring with anchors located outside the original terminal footprint.
Proponent states the spread mooring system 'will not restrict vessel movement in Douglas Channel' — amendment approval remains pending at time of writing.

financeRisk.text: Class III gross capital cost estimate (April 2024): US$3.4 billion. US$2.3 billion (~70%) is under a fixed-price lump-sum agreement for the FLNG production unit; US$1.1B is onshore infrastructure, owner's costs, commissioning, start-up and financial assurances. Total project cost including ≈ US$0.6B interest during construction and transaction costs ≈ US$4.0 billion (gross).

sources.citing: Pembina Apr 4 2024 — Capital Cost Estimate and Funding

financeRisk.text: Project financing structure ≈ 60% project debt / 40% equity. MUFG Bank, Ltd. engaged as financial advisor on project financing. EPC consortium = Samsung Heavy Industries (Korea) + Black & Veatch (LNG technology). Counsel: Baker Botts L.L.P. (commercial); King & Spalding LLP (EPC).

20-year take-or-pay liquefaction tolling agreement with ARC Resources Ltd. for 1.5 mtpa at a fixed toll; ARC supplies ≈ 200 mmcf/d of Montney gas via Coastal GasLink. Pembina holds a mirror bridging agreement for the remaining 1.5 mtpa and intends to assign that capacity to a third-party offtaker post-FID.

Federal committed capital ≈ $400M (CER aggregation) — $200M Strategic Innovation Fund + a First Nations Finance Authority capital commitment. BC provincial contribution of $200M specifically supports the electrification of Cedar LNG.

LNG price exposure: revenue depends on global LNG prices through offtake agreements (mitigated by 20-year take-or-pay). Sustained low-price environment would compress cash flows available to debt service and to Haisla's equity distribution.

Pembina's run-rate adjusted EBITDA guidance: US$200M–US$260M per year net to Pembina (50% share). Low end = take-or-pay only; high end includes incremental cargo and marketing upside. Comparable economics attributable to the Haisla 50.1% interest are material over the operating life.

The Morningstar DBRS October 2025 credit rating report on FNFA explicitly cites 'Haisla First Nation's majority equity ownership of the Cedar LNG project' as a driver of equity-participation loan demand growing FNFA's pool — third-party regulatory/credit-agency confirmation of the Haisla majority structure.

governanceQuestions: How are project-level decisions (capex deferrals, operating decisions, future expansions) governed between the 50.1% Indigenous equity holder and the operating partner?
What sequencing of debt repayment vs. community distribution applies, and how is it disclosed to Haisla members?
What independent governance and audit structures exist to ensure the partnership delivers as designed across the operating life?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: For Haisla members: in low / base / high LNG price scenarios, what is the annual expected distribution after debt service?
What is the long-tail decommissioning plan, and who carries the obligation?
How are operating-period environmental commitments (marine traffic, transmission corridor, electrification reliability) enforceable by the community?
What constraints (if any) does the financing place on the Haisla Nation's broader treaty-rights or governance positions outside this project?

finance.structure: Cedar LNG Partners LP. Haisla Nation 50.1%, Pembina 49.9%. Financing ≈ 60% project debt / 40% equity; MUFG project-finance advisor; CIBC advisor to Haisla on equity raise.
finance.totalCostEstimate: ≈ US$3.4B Class III estimate (≈ US$4.0B fully loaded with IDC)
finance.costOverrunsNoted: ~70% of gross capex under fixed-price lump-sum agreement (FLNG unit) reduces overrun exposure
finance.loanGuarantor: Federal: $200M Strategic Innovation Fund + FNFA capital commitment. Provincial: $200M BC contribution agreement for electrification.
finance.riskCarrier: Project equity holders bear operating and LNG-price risk; lenders bear default risk subject to federal participation; Haisla cash flow sits after debt service — meaningful in base/high price scenarios, more exposed in low scenarios.

primarySources.citing: Federal regulator confirmation of equity + capital structure
Third-party credit-rating citation of Cedar LNG as Indigenous-finance demand driver

projects.name: Coastal GasLink Pipeline
projects.summary: 670 km natural gas pipeline from Dawson Creek, BC to the LNG Canada export terminal at Kitimat. Now supplies Cedar LNG via ARC Resources' 200 mmcf/d Montney offtake. In service since 2023.
projects.location: Northern British Columbia (Dawson Creek to Kitimat)
projects.proponent: Coastal GasLink Pipeline Ltd. (TC Energy Corporation, with minority interests held by KKR-led consortium and Alberta Investment Management Corporation)
projects.governmentObjective: Enable LNG export from the Pacific coast as part of federal/provincial energy export strategy.
projects.proponentObjective: Long-term firm-service contract revenue from LNG Canada and Cedar LNG feedstock supply over the pipeline's operating life.

parties.name: 20 elected First Nation band councils along the route (impact-benefit and option agreements)

firstNationImplications.text: All 20 elected First Nation band councils whose reserve lands the route passes through signed project agreements; Wet'suwet'en Hereditary Chiefs have publicly opposed the pipeline across their unceded traditional territory and authority.

Delgamuukw recognized Wet'suwet'en oral histories of governance; Tsilhqot'in confirms title-holders' decision-making rights. The practical relationship between hereditary and elected authority for consent on this project remains legally and politically contested.

sources.citing: Aboriginal title affirms governance and decision-making over title lands

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Route crosses several salmon-bearing waterways; multiple BC EAO non-compliance orders have been issued during construction relating to sediment and erosion control.

Cumulative-effects exposure: this pipeline adds to a corridor of energy infrastructure that has been the subject of treaty-rights infringement findings elsewhere in BC (Yahey 2021 BCSC 1287 — Treaty 8).

sources.citing: Yahey precedent on cumulative effects in Treaty 8 territory

financeRisk.text: Reported construction cost grew to approximately CAD $14.5B, more than double the original ~$6.6B estimate. Cost overruns ultimately settled between TC Energy and LNG Canada shippers under confidential commercial arrangements.

FNMPC's 2024 primer documents that 'some First Nations attempted to acquire an additional 10% in a now-completed [CGL] financing round and had to forfeit their opportunity to their private-sector partners as they were unable to successfully raise capital at rates that made the equity investment financially viable'.

sources.citing: FNMPC Loan Guarantee Primer — CGL case study

financeRisk.text: The CGL forfeiture pre-dates federal CILGC ILGP operationalization (the federal Indigenous loan-guarantee program announced Fall 2023, with the v3 applicant guide dated 2026). The Coastal GasLink case is the canonical documented illustration that without the federal guarantee, capital-cost barriers translate into actual lost ownership opportunities.
FNMPC's January 2024 portfolio lists 'Coastal GasLink Equity Option (10%) $14.2B' as one of its 14 active support files.

governanceQuestions: Where the elected council and hereditary chiefs differ, what process determines whose consent governs for purposes of project decision-making?
How are cumulative environmental effects with adjacent projects assessed against treaty-rights infringement standards from Yahey?
For any subsequent CGL-related equity transaction, what financing structure (CILGC, FNFA, AIOC) would now be available that was not in the documented forfeited round?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: What share of project cash flow, after debt service and operating costs, actually reaches our community in years 1, 5, and 10?
If a future equity option is offered, what loan-guarantee structure (CILGC, AIOC, provincial) is available, and on what terms?
Who decides on environmental remediation if there is a future spill, and what are our enforcement rights?
Does signing an equity agreement affect future treaty-rights litigation we may want to bring?
What independent legal and financial review has our community had, and was it funded by the proponent?

finance.structure: Privately-held by TC Energy, a KKR-led consortium, and AIMCo. FNMPC documents a now-completed financing round in which some First Nations forfeited an additional 10% equity opportunity due to capital-cost barriers (pre-federal-ILGP).
finance.loanGuarantor: No federal Indigenous loan guarantee was in place during the documented forfeiture round.
finance.riskCarrier: Construction-cost overruns borne by TC Energy and resolved with shippers. The forfeited Indigenous equity opportunity is borne by the communities that could not finance it — the documented cost of inaction.

primarySources.citing: FNMPC primer — CGL forfeiture case study
Federal ILGP that did not exist at time of forfeiture

projects.name: Site C Clean Energy Project
projects.summary: 1,100 MW hydroelectric dam on the Peace River in northeast British Columbia. Reservoir filling underway since August 2024 (62 km upstream of the Alberta border).
projects.location: Peace River, near Fort St. John, BC (Treaty 8 territory)
projects.governmentObjective: Add ~5,100 GWh/year of firm hydroelectric capacity to the BC grid to meet electrification, industrial, and population growth demand.
projects.proponentObjective: Deliver large-scale firm baseload generation that integrates with BC's existing hydro system.

firstNationImplications.text: West Moberly and Prophet River First Nations brought a treaty infringement claim against the project; the claim was settled in 2022 prior to a trial decision. The settlement is confidential — its precedent value is therefore limited.

The Blueberry River Implementation Agreement (January 2023) explicitly carves out electricity transmission and distribution rights-of-way outside Area 1 — or inside Area 1 with Blueberry consent — from the definition of 'New Disturbance'. This was a deliberate accommodation enabling Site C transmission corridors to proceed in their current form post-Yahey.

sources.citing: Blueberry River Implementation Agreement — PNG / Site C carveout

firstNationImplications.text: Reservoir flooding affects burial sites, fishing sites, hunting territories, and cultural sites. The long-term cultural-loss impact extends across generations and is not fully quantified in the public record.

sources.citing: Treaty 8 hunting/fishing/trapping rights affirmed in Yahey

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Following the 2021 Yahey decision in favour of Blueberry River First Nations on cumulative effects in the same region, BC's broader approach to authorizing development in Treaty 8 territory has been under sustained legal and political pressure.

Reservoir conversion of riverine ecosystem to lake-like conditions affects sediment transport, fish habitat, and the downstream flow regime for decades. Site C reservoir filling began August 2024 (per AB GoA tracking); transboundary flow effects are rated 'low' impact in the MPWA 2024 Peace & Slave Watershed report but actively monitored.

financeRisk.text: Capital cost grew from a 2014 estimate of CAD $7.9B to approximately CAD $16B (BC Hydro's 2021 reforecast).
Cost is ultimately borne by BC ratepayers through electricity rates; the Province of BC has provided rate-relief mechanisms.
If long-run electricity demand falls short of projections (e.g. industrial load loss), the cost-recovery burden per ratepayer rises.

governanceQuestions: What public-interest test would a similarly situated project face today under DRIPA and the post-Yahey consultation framework?
What governance role do affected First Nations have over reservoir-related decisions (ramping, debris management, future cultural-site protection) for the operating life of the dam?
How does the Blueberry River Implementation Agreement's Site C transmission carveout interact with future treaty-rights claims by other Treaty 8 First Nations?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: What enforceable commitments exist around access to traditional territory now under reservoir?
What is the long-term flow regime, and what monitoring will affected communities have access to?
What is the precedent value of the West Moberly / Prophet River settlement for future projects in our territory?

finance.structure: Crown corporation balance sheet; ultimately backstopped by Province of BC.
finance.loanGuarantor: Province of British Columbia (Crown corporation debt).
finance.riskCarrier: BC ratepayers via electricity rates; with Provincial backstop.

projects.name: Trans Mountain Expansion Project (TMX)
projects.summary: Twinned crude-oil pipeline from Edmonton, AB to Burnaby, BC, nearly tripling nameplate capacity from ~300,000 to ~890,000 bbl/day. In service since May 2024.
projects.location: Edmonton, AB to Burnaby, BC (with Westridge Marine Terminal expansion)
projects.proponent: Trans Mountain Corporation (federal Crown corporation since 2018)
projects.governmentObjective: Provide tidewater access for Alberta heavy crude and reduce price discounts on Canadian crude.
projects.proponentObjective: Operate the pipeline to recover capital cost via committed shipper tolls under regulated tariffs.

parties.name: 129 Indigenous groups along the route (per Trans Mountain Corp.)
Government of Canada (sole shareholder via Canada TMP Finance Ltd.)

firstNationImplications.text: The Federal Court of Appeal in 2018 (Tsleil-Waututh Nation v. Canada) found that the second-round Phase III consultation was inadequate and quashed the original federal approval; the project was re-approved in 2019 after a remedial consultation process.

sources.citing: Duty-to-consult standard applied in TMX litigation

firstNationImplications.text: Multiple First Nation-led equity acquisition proposals (e.g. Project Reconciliation, Nesika, Iron Coalition) have been publicly discussed; no transaction has been announced as of this writing.

The closest documented analogue for an Indigenous-led pipeline-equity acquisition is the Athabasca Indigenous Investments transaction: $1.12B raised for an 11.57% Enbridge Oilsands stake — first $250M ATB Financial loan was guaranteed by AIOC; remaining $870M raised via bond. Demonstrates that the loan-guarantee architecture can support Indigenous equity in operating pipelines at scale.

sources.citing: FNMPC primer — Athabasca Indigenous Investments / Enbridge transaction
CER 2026 — Enbridge Oilsands Lines / Athabasca Indigenous Investments 11.57%

firstNationImplications.text: If a sale to an Indigenous-led consortium were structured with CILGC ILGP support, per-deal headroom would be up to $1B (with a 75% federal stacking cap if combined with CIB or other federal Crown exposure). Specific TMX divestment terms remain open.

sources.citing: CILGC ILGP — per-deal maximum and stacking cap

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Tanker traffic in Burrard Inlet associated with the expanded Westridge Marine Terminal raises documented marine-spill and Southern Resident Killer Whale concerns that were central to the 2018 FCA decision.
Coldwater Indian Band raised specific concerns about a routing alignment over their sole-source aquifer; the federal regulator and Trans Mountain have engaged on routing alternatives.

financeRisk.text: Capital cost grew from a 2017 estimate of ≈ CAD $7.4B to approximately CAD $34B at completion (Trans Mountain Corporation disclosure).
Government of Canada has signalled intent to divest its interest in the project; the eventual sale price, buyer composition, and Indigenous participation terms remain open.
If tolls or shipper-commitment volumes prove insufficient to recover the full capital cost, the federal Crown carries the residual exposure until a divestment closes.

governanceQuestions: What is the federal government's framework for evaluating Indigenous-led acquisition proposals, and how is fair price determined when the seller is the Crown and the buyer is partly Crown-financed?
How will environmental obligations (decommissioning, spill response, marine impacts) be preserved across a change of ownership?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: If our Nation is offered a stake in a future Indigenous-led acquisition, what is the cash-flow waterfall — who gets paid first, and where do we sit?
What is the indemnity structure for legacy environmental liabilities — do they stay with the Crown, transfer to buyers, or get split?
What veto, observer, or governance rights does an equity stake convey, distinct from cash returns?

finance.structure: Federal Crown corporation. Acquired by Canada in 2018 for CAD $4.5B. Construction completed in 2024 at ≈ CAD $34B.
finance.loanGuarantor: Government of Canada (via Canada TMP Finance Ltd.).
finance.riskCarrier: Government of Canada (federal taxpayers) until a divestment closes; tolls and shipper commitments are the recovery mechanism.

primarySources.citing: Federal ILGP framework relevant to a future Indigenous-led TMX acquisition
